verses.text: Moreover he made an altar of brass, twenty cubits the length thereof, and twenty cubits the breadth thereof, and ten cubits the height thereof. Also he made a molten sea of ten cubits from brim to brim, round in compass, and five cubits the height thereof; and a line of thirty cubits did compass it round about. And under it was the similitude of oxen, which did compass it round about: ten in a cubit, compassing the sea round about. Two rows of oxen were cast, when it was cast. It stood upon twelve oxen, three looking toward the north, and three looking toward the west, and three looking toward the south, and three looking toward the east: and the sea was set above upon them, and all their hinder parts were inward. And the thickness of it was an handbreadth, and the brim of it like the work of the brim of a cup, with flowers of lilies; and it received and held three thousand baths. Guidance: The Chronicler describes two great furnishings of the temple court: the large brazen altar, and the molten sea, a vast bronze basin standing upon twelve oxen facing the four directions. These two together preach the way a sinner approaches a holy God. The altar was for sacrifice — the place where atonement was made, where blood was shed for sin. The sea was for washing — the place of cleansing, where the priests and the offerings were made clean. The chapter is showing us the two great necessities of all who would draw near to God: atonement and cleansing, the blood and the water. The chapter is teaching us that we cannot come to God uncleansed and unatoned; first the altar, where sin is dealt with by sacrifice, and then the laver, where defilement is washed away. These speak to us of the gospel, for from the side of Christ flowed both blood and water (John 19:34) — atonement for our guilt and cleansing for our pollution. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD has provided fully for sinners to approach Him: a way to be forgiven and a way to be made clean. The chapter is teaching us, in the great sea set upon twelve oxen looking toward every direction, an image of the means of cleansing supported and carried out toward all the world — some have seen in the twelve oxen a picture of the tribes, or of the apostles, bearing the water of cleansing to the four corners of the earth. The chapter is teaching us that the provision for cleansing is abundant — the sea held three thousand baths, a vast supply — for the grace of God to cleanse is more than sufficient for all who come. Let us learn from the altar and the sea that we approach God only through atonement and cleansing, both freely provided in Christ, and let us come boldly, our guilt covered by the blood and our defilement washed in the water of His grace.

He made also ten lavers, and put five on the right hand, and five on the left, to wash in them: such things as they offered for the burnt offering they washed in them; but the sea was for the priests to wash in. And he made ten candlesticks of gold according to their form, and set them in the temple, five on the right hand, and five on the left. He made also ten tables, and placed them in the temple, five on the right side, and five on the left. And he made an hundred basons of gold. Furthermore he made the court of the priests, and the great court, and doors for the court, and overlaid the doors of them with brass. And he set the sea on the right side of the east end, over against the south. Guidance: Solomon furnishes the temple richly: ten lavers for washing the sacrifices, ten golden candlesticks, ten tables, a hundred golden basins, and the courts with their bronze-overlaid doors. The chapter is showing us the abundant provision for every part of the worship of God — vessels for cleansing, lights for illumination, tables for the bread, all in generous number and of precious materials. The chapter is teaching us, in the ten candlesticks, that the house of God is to be full of light; the golden lamps illuminated the holy place, a picture of the light of God's truth and presence shining among His people. The chapter is teaching us, in the ten tables (for the shewbread), of the bread of God's provision ever set before Him, the LORD feeding and sustaining His people. The chapter is teaching us, in the lavers for washing the offerings, the constant theme of purity in approaching God — even the sacrifices were to be washed and made clean. There is a holy thoroughness here: nothing brought to God carelessly, but all washed, ordered, prepared. The chapter is teaching us that the worship of God involves light (His truth illuminating us), bread (His provision sustaining us), and cleansing (His purifying us), all richly supplied. The chapter is teaching us, too, in the courts for the priests and the people, that there is an order of approach to God — degrees of nearness, the priests' court and the great court — reminding us of the reverent structure of worship and the privilege, now ours in Christ, of drawing near with boldness into the very holiest. The chapter is teaching us that God provides abundantly for the worship of His people — light, bread, cleansing, and a place to draw near — and that all these earthly furnishings point to the spiritual realities we enjoy in Him who is our Light, our Bread, our Cleansing, and our Way to the Father.

And Huram made the pots, and the shovels, and the basons. And Huram finished the work that he was to make for king Solomon for the house of God; To wit, the two pillars, and the pommels, and the chapiters which were on the top of the two pillars, and the two wreaths to cover the two pommels of the chapiters which were on the top of the pillars; And four hundred pomegranates on the two wreaths; two rows of pomegranates on each wreath, to cover the two pommels of the chapiters which were upon the pillars. He made also bases, and lavers made he upon the bases; One sea, and twelve oxen under it. The pots also, and the shovels, and the fleshhooks, and all their instruments, did Huram his father make to king Solomon for the house of the LORD of bright brass. In the plain of Jordan did the king cast them, in the clay ground between Succoth and Zeredathah. Thus Solomon made all these vessels in great abundance: for the weight of the brass could not be found out. Guidance: Huram, the skilled craftsman, completes all the bronze work — the pillars, the wreaths and pomegranates, the bases, the sea, the pots, shovels, and instruments, all of 'bright brass' cast in the clay ground of the Jordan plain. And the Chronicler marvels: 'Solomon made all these vessels in great abundance: for the weight of the brass could not be found out.' The chapter is showing us the faithful completion of a great work; Huram 'finished the work that he was to make,' bringing his skilled labour to its appointed end. The chapter is teaching us the value of finishing what we begin in God's service — Huram did not leave the work half-done, but completed every vessel he was charged to make. There is honour in the faithful finishing of a task. The chapter is teaching us, in the unsearchable weight of the brass, the lavish abundance with which the house of God was furnished; so much was given and used that it could not be weighed. When God's people gave themselves and their resources wholeheartedly to His house, the supply was beyond counting. The chapter is teaching us that generosity toward God's work, when the heart is willing, overflows all reckoning — 'the weight of the brass could not be found out.' The chapter is teaching us the dignity of skilled craftsmanship offered to God; Huram, a man of mixed and partly foreign descent, devoted his great skill to the house of the LORD and finished his work well, his name honoured forever in Scripture. The chapter is teaching us to bring our skill, our diligence, and our abundance to the work of God, and to finish faithfully what He gives us to do — for the LORD honours both the willing abundance of His people and the faithful completion of every appointed task in the building of His house.

The chapter is teaching us to bring our skill, our diligence, and our abundance to the work of God, and to finish faithfully what He gives us to do — for the LORD honours both the willing abundance of His people and the faithful completion of every appointed task in the building of His house.

And Huram made the pots, and the shovels, and the basons. And Huram finished the work that he was to make for king Solomon for the house of God; To wit, the two pillars, and the pommels, and the chapiters which were on the top of the two pillars, and the two wreaths to cover the two pommels of the chapiters which were on the top of the pillars; And four hundred pomegranates on the two wreaths; two rows of pomegranates on each wreath, to cover the two pommels of the chapiters which were upon the pillars. He made also bases, and lavers made he upon the bases; One sea, and twelve oxen under it. The pots also, and the shovels, and the fleshhooks, and all their instruments, did Huram his father make to king Solomon for the house of the LORD of bright brass. In the plain of Jordan did the king cast them, in the clay ground between Succoth and Zeredathah. Thus Solomon made all these vessels in great abundance: for the weight of the brass could not be found out. Guidance: Huram, the skilled craftsman, completes all the bronze work — the pillars, the wreaths and pomegranates, the bases, the sea, the pots, shovels, and instruments, all of 'bright brass' cast in the clay ground of the Jordan plain. And the Chronicler marvels: 'Solomon made all these vessels in great abundance: for the weight of the brass could not be found out.' The chapter is showing us the faithful completion of a great work; Huram 'finished the work that he was to make,' bringing his skilled labour to its appointed end. The chapter is teaching us the value of finishing what we begin in God's service — Huram did not leave the work half-done, but completed every vessel he was charged to make. There is honour in the faithful finishing of a task. The chapter is teaching us, in the unsearchable weight of the brass, the lavish abundance with which the house of God was furnished; so much was given and used that it could not be weighed. When God's people gave themselves and their resources wholeheartedly to His house, the supply was beyond counting. The chapter is teaching us that generosity toward God's work, when the heart is willing, overflows all reckoning — 'the weight of the brass could not be found out.' The chapter is teaching us the dignity of skilled craftsmanship offered to God; Huram, a man of mixed and partly foreign descent, devoted his great skill to the house of the LORD and finished his work well, his name honoured forever in Scripture. The chapter is teaching us to bring our skill, our diligence, and our abundance to the work of God, and to finish faithfully what He gives us to do — for the LORD honours both the willing abundance of His people and the faithful completion of every appointed task in the building of His house.

And Solomon made all the vessels that were for the house of God, the golden altar also, and the tables whereon the shewbread was set; Moreover the candlesticks with their lamps, that they should burn after the manner before the oracle, of pure gold; And the flowers, and the lamps, and the tongs, made he of gold, and that perfect gold; And the snuffers, and the basons, and the spoons, and the censers, of pure gold: and the entry of the house, the inner doors thereof for the most holy place, and the doors of the house of the temple, were of gold. Guidance: The Chronicler turns from the bronze of the courts to the gold of the inner house: the golden altar of incense, the tables of shewbread, the candlesticks 'of pure gold,' the flowers, lamps, tongs, snuffers, basins, spoons, and censers — all of 'perfect gold' — and even the doors of the most holy place and of the temple itself were gold. The chapter is showing us a striking pattern: the nearer to the most holy place, the more precious the material; the outer courts had bronze, but the inner house gleamed everywhere with pure gold. The chapter is teaching us that the closer we come to the presence of God, the greater the glory; the innermost place was all gold, for it was nearest the throne of the LORD. So in the life of faith, the deeper we draw near to God, the more we behold and are changed by His glory. The chapter is teaching us that nothing but the purest and best was used in the place closest to God — 'perfect gold' — for His immediate presence demands the highest glory we can offer. The chapter is teaching us, in the golden altar of incense, of the prayers of the saints rising before God (Rev 8:3-4); in the shewbread tables, of His provision; in the candlesticks, of His light — all now in pure gold, the most glorious form. The chapter is teaching us that the worship of God deserves our very best, and that the things nearest His presence should be the most precious of all. The chapter is teaching us to give God the gold of our lives — our purest devotion, our best and most refined offerings — especially in the inner sanctuary of the heart, where we draw nearest to Him. The whole golden interior of the temple preached one truth: the presence of God is glorious beyond all else, and worthy of the purest gold we can bring; and it pointed forward to the true and heavenly sanctuary, where we shall behold a glory of which all this gold was but a shadow.
